name: Félix Giorgetti Node.js Interview Preparation
description: Targeted spoken technical interview questions based only on the Experienced Node.js Developer job advertisement: Node.js, APIs, data stores, messaging, event-driven systems, production troubleshooting, CI/CD, serverless, legacy .NET integration, and ownership.

topics.name: Node.js Runtime Concurrency
topics.description: How Node.js handles concurrent work with the event loop, asynchronous I/O, and worker threads.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Node.js runs JavaScript on a main thread and uses an event loop to coordinate many operations. Network and file operations can continue outside the JavaScript call stack, so one process can keep many requests in flight without creating one thread per request. This is concurrency, not automatic parallel execution of JavaScript.

## What problem does it solve?

This model is efficient for I/O-heavy services, but it becomes fragile when code blocks the main thread. A long CPU calculation, synchronous file operation, or very large JSON parse can delay every request handled by that process. Interviewers want to know whether you understand both the strength and the limit of Node.js.

## How it works

The call stack runs JavaScript until it is empty. Completed timers, I/O callbacks, and promise continuations are scheduled for later processing. Promise callbacks use the microtask queue and normally run before the next timer or I/O phase.

Use asynchronous APIs for I/O. Split or move CPU-heavy work to worker threads, a job queue, or another service. Run multiple Node.js processes or containers to use multiple CPU cores and provide isolation. Measure event-loop delay because low CPU usage alone does not prove that the service is responsive.

## Common mistakes

- Saying Node.js is single-threaded and therefore handles only one request at a time
- Assuming async code makes CPU-heavy work non-blocking
- Using synchronous filesystem or crypto APIs in request handlers
- Adding more promises without measuring event-loop delay

## Key terms

- event loop — scheduler that keeps processing ready JavaScript work
- non-blocking I/O — I/O that does not hold the JavaScript thread while waiting
- microtask — high-priority continuation used by promises
- worker thread — separate thread for CPU-heavy JavaScript work
- event-loop delay — time ready work waits before JavaScript can run

topics.name: Node.js Memory Diagnostics
topics.description: A practical method for finding memory leaks and excessive memory use in long-running Node.js services.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Node.js memory diagnostics is the process of finding why a service keeps using more memory or is killed with an out-of-memory error. A leak means objects remain reachable even though the application no longer needs them. High memory use is not always a leak; it may also come from large buffers, traffic spikes, or an undersized limit.

## What problem does it solve?

A leaking service becomes slower because garbage collection works harder, then restarts or crashes. Blindly increasing the memory limit may only delay the failure. A structured investigation helps distinguish a real leak from normal caching, temporary load, or native memory outside the JavaScript heap.

## How it works

Start with a timeline: process RSS, JavaScript heap used, heap limit, request rate, and garbage-collection activity. Reproduce the growth under controlled load when possible. Take heap snapshots at comparable points and compare retained objects and retaining paths.

Common causes include unbounded maps, caches without eviction, event listeners never removed, timers, closures that retain large objects, unresolved promises, and buffers held by streams. Check listener counts and cache cardinality. Use allocation profiling for code that steadily creates retained objects. Verify the fix with the same load and a soak test rather than only observing one restart.

## Common mistakes

- Calling every temporary memory spike a leak
- Looking only at heap used and ignoring RSS or buffers
- Taking one heap snapshot with nothing to compare
- Increasing the memory limit without finding the retaining reference

## Key terms

- heap snapshot — captured graph of JavaScript objects and references
- retaining path — reference chain that keeps an object alive
- RSS — total resident memory used by the process
- garbage collection — automatic recovery of unreachable memory
- soak test — sustained test that reveals slow growth over time

topics.name: Node.js Performance Diagnostics
topics.description: How to isolate latency and throughput bottlenecks in a Node.js service.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Node.js performance diagnostics is a measurement-driven process for explaining slow responses, low throughput, high CPU, or event-loop stalls. It separates symptoms from causes across the application, database, network, and dependencies.

## What problem does it solve?

Without a method, teams often optimize the wrong layer or add servers to hide an inefficient query. A useful investigation identifies which requests are affected, where time is spent, and whether the limit is CPU, I/O, contention, capacity, or an external dependency.

## How it works

Begin with user-facing latency percentiles, error rate, traffic, and saturation. Compare p50, p95, and p99 rather than relying on averages. Use request traces to break total latency into database calls, external APIs, queue waits, and application work.

For Node.js, measure event-loop delay, CPU profiles, garbage-collection pauses, heap pressure, and active handles. For databases, inspect query plans, indexes, lock waits, and connection-pool usage. Reproduce the issue with representative payloads and concurrency. Change one thing, measure again, and confirm that the improvement helps the original service-level objective without creating a new bottleneck.

## Common mistakes

- Optimizing code before locating where time is spent
- Using average latency and hiding slow tail requests
- Running unrealistic load tests with tiny payloads
- Scaling horizontally while a shared database is already saturated

## Key terms

- latency percentile — threshold below which a percentage of requests finish
- saturation — resource operating near its useful capacity
- CPU profile — sample showing where processor time is spent
- distributed trace — request timeline across services
- event-loop delay — wait before ready JavaScript work can execute

topics.name: Messaging Pattern Selection
topics.description: Choosing between queues, publish-subscribe, topics, and request-reply messaging.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Messaging patterns describe how producers and consumers exchange work or information. A queue normally distributes each message to one worker. Publish-subscribe sends the same event to multiple interested consumers. Request-reply asks for a response through messaging, while topics route messages by a key or subject.

## What problem does it solve?

Choosing the wrong pattern creates hidden coupling, duplicate work, or missing consumers. For example, a business event placed on a competing-consumer queue may reach only one service even though several services need it. Request-reply over a broker can also reproduce synchronous coupling with extra complexity.

## How it works

Start from the business meaning. Use a work queue when one of several workers should complete a task. Use publish-subscribe when each subscriber needs its own copy of an event. Use topics or routing keys when subscribers need filtered subsets.

Decide whether the sender needs an immediate answer. Synchronous request-reply is simpler for short, required interactions. Asynchronous messaging is useful for buffering, fan-out, and independent availability, but requires correlation identifiers, retries, duplicate handling, and observability. Also define ownership: a command asks one owner to do something, while an event states that something already happened.

## Common mistakes

- Treating a queue and publish-subscribe as interchangeable
- Using asynchronous request-reply for every service call
- Publishing commands as if they were neutral business events
- Ignoring duplicate delivery and correlation

## Key terms

- queue — channel where one consumer handles each message
- publish-subscribe — each subscription receives its own event copy
- topic — named or keyed route for related messages
- command — request for one owner to perform an action
- event — fact that something has already happened

topics.name: Message Broker Selection
topics.description: How workload and operational needs influence a choice between NATS, RabbitMQ, and Kafka.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Message broker selection means matching a communication platform to the required behavior rather than choosing by popularity. NATS, RabbitMQ, and Kafka overlap, but their strongest use cases and operational models differ.

## What problem does it solve?

A poor choice can make retention, routing, replay, or scaling unnecessarily difficult. Interviewers usually do not expect product trivia. They expect clear decision criteria and awareness that configuration, persistence mode, and managed services can change the trade-offs.

## How it works

NATS is often chosen for lightweight, low-latency messaging and simple subjects. JetStream adds persistence, replay, and stronger delivery features. RabbitMQ is strong for work queues, acknowledgements, flexible routing through exchanges, and per-message workflows. Kafka is a distributed append-only log designed for high-throughput event streams, retained history, replay, and partitioned consumer groups.

Compare required retention, replay, routing complexity, ordering scope, throughput, message size, delivery guarantees, operational skill, and ecosystem integration. Also ask whether the company already operates one platform successfully. The best technology is often the one that meets the requirement with the lowest total operational risk.

## Common mistakes

- Claiming one broker is always faster or always better
- Choosing Kafka for a small task queue only because it scales
- Ignoring retention and replay requirements
- Comparing product names without discussing workload

## Key terms

- subject — NATS routing name
- exchange — RabbitMQ routing component
- partition — ordered Kafka log segment
- consumer group — consumers sharing partitions
- retention — how long messages remain available

topics.name: Message Delivery Semantics
topics.description: At-most-once, at-least-once, and effectively-once processing in distributed messaging.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Delivery semantics describe what can happen when a broker or consumer fails around message processing. At-most-once can lose messages but avoids redelivery. At-least-once avoids loss by retrying, so duplicates are possible. Exactly-once is usually limited to a defined system boundary and is often implemented as effectively-once business behavior.

## What problem does it solve?

Failures can occur after a side effect but before the acknowledgement reaches the broker. The broker then redelivers a message that may already have changed a database or called another service. A correct design assumes this uncertainty instead of treating duplicates as exceptional.

## How it works

For at-least-once delivery, make handlers idempotent. Use a stable message or operation identifier and store processed identifiers or enforce a unique business constraint. Commit the side effect and deduplication record in the same local transaction when possible. Acknowledge only after successful processing.

Retries need limits, backoff, and classification of transient versus permanent errors. For database changes plus event publishing, use an outbox pattern rather than an unsafe dual write. Explain the exact boundary when using the phrase exactly-once; a broker guarantee does not automatically cover external databases or APIs.

## Common mistakes

- Promising exactly-once across every external system
- Acknowledging before the business transaction completes
- Retrying non-idempotent operations without a key
- Treating duplicate messages as a broker bug

## Key terms

- at-most-once — no redelivery, with possible loss
- at-least-once — retry on uncertainty, with possible duplicates
- idempotency key — stable identifier used to suppress repeated effects
- acknowledgement — consumer confirmation that processing succeeded
- effectively-once — duplicate delivery with one business outcome

topics.name: Message Ordering
topics.description: How partitions and keys provide limited ordering in distributed event systems.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Message ordering is the guarantee that consumers observe related events in a defined sequence. Distributed brokers rarely provide one global order at unlimited scale. They normally guarantee order only within a queue, partition, subject, or consumer stream.

## What problem does it solve?

Business processes can break when an update is handled before a creation event or when two changes to the same entity are applied in reverse order. At the same time, forcing every event through one ordered channel can become a throughput bottleneck.

## How it works

Define what must be ordered. Usually events for the same customer, order, or account need order, while unrelated entities can be processed independently. Use that entity identifier as a partition or routing key so related events reach the same ordered stream.

Consumers should still handle duplicates and delayed events. Add event versions, sequence numbers, or timestamps when the domain needs stale-update detection. Repartitioning and increasing consumer parallelism can change assignment, so understand the broker's behavior during rebalancing. Avoid global ordering unless the business truly requires it, because one partition limits parallelism and availability.

## Common mistakes

- Assuming a broker guarantees global order by default
- Using a random partition key for entity updates
- Equating delivery order with exactly-once processing
- Forcing one partition without discussing throughput

## Key terms

- partition key — value that selects the ordered stream
- sequence number — monotonic value used to detect stale events
- rebalance — reassignment of partitions between consumers
- per-key ordering — order guaranteed only for one routing key
- global ordering — one sequence for all events

topics.name: Dead-Letter Handling
topics.description: How to isolate, inspect, and recover messages that repeatedly fail processing.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Dead-letter handling moves messages that cannot be processed after an allowed number of attempts into a separate queue or stream. These messages are often called poison messages because retrying them forever blocks progress or wastes resources.

## What problem does it solve?

A dead-letter queue prevents infinite retry loops, but it is not a complete solution. Failed messages still represent lost business progress. The team needs enough context to understand the failure, correct the cause, and safely replay or compensate.

## How it works

Classify errors before retrying. Temporary dependency failures may succeed with exponential backoff and jitter. Validation errors, missing required data, or an unsupported schema are usually permanent until something changes. After the retry limit, preserve the original payload, message identifier, error reason, attempt count, timestamps, and relevant trace identifiers.

Alert on dead-letter growth and age, not only individual messages. Provide a controlled replay path that retains idempotency and avoids flooding the recovered dependency. Some cases need a business compensation rather than replay. Access to payloads must follow security and privacy rules.

## Common mistakes

- Using a dead-letter queue as permanent storage
- Retrying every error with the same policy
- Replaying messages without idempotency protection
- Creating a dead-letter queue without monitoring or ownership

## Key terms

- poison message — message that repeatedly fails processing
- dead-letter queue — isolated destination for failed messages
- retry budget — maximum attempts or elapsed retry time
- replay — controlled reprocessing of stored failed messages
- compensation — business action that corrects an incomplete workflow

topics.name: Event Schema Evolution
topics.description: How to change event contracts without breaking independent producers and consumers.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Event schema evolution is the controlled change of fields, meanings, and versions in messages that may be stored or consumed by independently deployed services. Events live longer than a normal function call and may be replayed months later.

## What problem does it solve?

Breaking a schema can stop consumers, corrupt projections, or make historical replay impossible. Producers and consumers are often deployed at different times, so the contract must tolerate a period when old and new versions coexist.

## How it works

Prefer additive changes: add optional fields with safe defaults and keep existing meanings stable. Consumers should ignore fields they do not understand and handle missing optional fields. Do not silently reuse a field with a new meaning.

For incompatible changes, publish a new event type or explicit major version and migrate consumers gradually. A schema registry and compatibility checks in CI can enforce rules. Contract tests verify representative producer and consumer expectations. Include metadata such as event type, version, identifier, occurrence time, and correlation identifier. Keep business events focused on stable domain facts rather than exposing an internal database row.

## Common mistakes

- Renaming or deleting a field because all current consumers were updated
- Changing the meaning of an existing field without a new version
- Assuming only the latest event version will ever be replayed
- Publishing raw database entities as permanent contracts

## Key terms

- backward compatibility — new consumer can read older events
- forward compatibility — old consumer tolerates newer events
- schema registry — shared store and compatibility gate for schemas
- upcaster — code that converts an older event representation
- contract test — automated check of producer-consumer expectations

topics.name: Serverless Function Trade-offs
topics.description: When Azure Functions or similar serverless compute is useful and when another runtime is safer.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A serverless function is code executed on demand while the cloud provider manages the underlying servers and scaling. Azure Functions can be triggered by HTTP requests, timers, queues, events, and other Azure services. Serverless removes server management, not application architecture or operational responsibility.

## What problem does it solve?

Functions are attractive for bursty workloads, event handlers, scheduled jobs, and small integration components. They can be a poor fit for long-running work, strict low-latency requirements, large stateful processes, or workloads with steady high utilization.

## How it works

Evaluate trigger type, execution limits, concurrency, cold-start tolerance, networking, state, observability, and cost at expected traffic. Keep handlers stateless and move durable state to a database, queue, or workflow service. Make event-triggered functions idempotent because retries are normal.

Cold starts can be reduced with suitable plans or pre-warmed capacity, but this changes cost. Durable Functions can coordinate multi-step workflows, though they add platform-specific behavior. Compare a function with a container or long-running service based on total lifecycle: deployment, debugging, scaling, local development, and operational ownership.

## Common mistakes

- Assuming serverless means free or infinitely scalable
- Keeping important state in function memory
- Ignoring cold starts and execution limits
- Using one large function as a hidden monolith

## Key terms

- trigger — event that starts a function invocation
- cold start — startup delay for a new execution environment
- stateless handler — invocation that does not rely on local memory
- consumption plan — pay-per-use execution model
- durable workflow — persisted coordination of function steps

topics.name: CI/CD Release Safety
topics.description: Building a delivery pipeline that catches defects and supports controlled deployment and rollback.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

CI/CD release safety is the set of automated checks and deployment controls that move a change from commit to production with known risk. Continuous integration validates small changes frequently. Continuous delivery keeps a releasable artifact ready and uses a repeatable process to deploy it.

## What problem does it solve?

A pipeline that only builds code is not enough. Backend releases can fail because of incompatible APIs, unsafe database migrations, missing configuration, or behavior that appears only under real traffic. Safe delivery limits the blast radius and makes recovery fast.

## How it works

Build one immutable artifact and promote the same artifact through environments. Typical gates include linting, unit tests, integration tests, contract tests, dependency scanning, and configuration validation. Apply database changes with backward-compatible expand-and-contract steps.

Use health checks and post-deployment metrics. Gradual strategies such as canary or blue-green releases expose only part of the traffic first. Define automatic or manual rollback criteria, but remember that a database migration may require a forward fix instead of code rollback. Record who deployed what and keep secrets outside the artifact.

## Common mistakes

- Rebuilding a different artifact for each environment
- Treating successful deployment as proof of correct behavior
- Deploying a breaking schema change before compatible code
- Planning rollback without considering data changes

## Key terms

- immutable artifact — unchanged build promoted between environments
- canary release — small traffic share receives the new version first
- blue-green — switch traffic between two complete environments
- quality gate — automated condition required before promotion
- expand-contract — staged backward-compatible schema migration

topics.name: Legacy Component Integration
topics.description: How to maintain and gradually replace components written in another platform such as .NET.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Legacy component integration connects a modern service to an existing component that cannot be replaced immediately. The component may use another language, framework, database, protocol, or deployment process. The goal is safe cooperation while reducing coupling and migration risk.

## What problem does it solve?

A rewrite is rarely an instant replacement. Existing behavior may be poorly documented, and business users still depend on it. Directly sharing internal classes or database tables creates tight coupling and makes both systems harder to change.

## How it works

Start by documenting the current contract, data ownership, failure modes, and operational limits. Prefer a stable boundary such as REST, messaging, or a small adapter service. Validate inputs and translate between internal models at the boundary.

Use the strangler pattern when replacing functionality gradually: route one capability at a time to the new implementation while the old component continues handling the rest. Add characterization tests around existing behavior before changing it. Monitor both paths and provide a rollback route. Shared databases should be a temporary compromise with explicit ownership, because schema changes otherwise coordinate every deployment.

## Common mistakes

- Starting a full rewrite without understanding existing behavior
- Sharing one database and calling the systems independent
- Leaking platform-specific models through the public contract
- Migrating all traffic at once without comparison or rollback

## Key terms

- adapter — boundary translating one interface or model to another
- strangler pattern — incremental replacement around a legacy system
- characterization test — test that records current behavior
- anti-corruption layer — translation that protects a new domain model
- data ownership — service responsible for changing a data set

topics.name: Technical Debt Prioritization
topics.description: How to identify and reduce technical debt according to business and production risk.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Technical debt is the future cost created by a design, implementation, or operational shortcut. Some debt is a deliberate trade-off made to deliver sooner. Other debt appears through changing requirements, weak ownership, or repeated local fixes.

## What problem does it solve?

A team cannot remove every imperfection. Treating all cleanup as equally urgent creates endless refactoring, while ignoring debt increases incident risk and slows every later change. Prioritization connects technical work to measurable consequences.

## How it works

Describe the debt in terms of impact: production failures, security exposure, delivery delay, support cost, or inability to scale. Estimate likelihood, severity, and how often engineers touch the affected area. Track a small set of concrete items rather than a vague cleanup backlog.

Fix critical risk immediately. For recurring friction, reserve capacity or include the improvement in the next feature that touches the area. Use incremental refactoring with tests instead of large rewrites when possible. Communicate the trade-off in plain language and state what happens if the work is delayed. Verify the result through fewer incidents, shorter lead time, or reduced operational effort.

## Common mistakes

- Calling every disliked implementation technical debt
- Asking for a rewrite without measurable impact
- Hiding debt until it causes an incident
- Refactoring without tests or a safe migration path

## Key terms

- principal — continuing cost paid because debt remains
- interest — repeated extra effort caused by the debt
- risk matrix — likelihood and impact used for prioritization
- incremental refactoring — small behavior-preserving improvements
- lead time — time from change request to production

topics.name: Project Ownership
topics.description: Taking a backend change from unclear requirements through production operation and maintenance.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Project ownership means being responsible for the outcome of a change, not only for writing code. It covers understanding the problem, choosing a proportionate design, coordinating dependencies, delivering safely, and supporting the result in production.

## What problem does it solve?

Features fail when assumptions remain hidden, operational work is postponed, or teams hand problems to each other. Ownership creates a clear path from a business need to a maintained service with measurable behavior.

## How it works

Clarify users, expected behavior, constraints, failure cases, security needs, data ownership, and non-functional requirements. Write down important decisions and alternatives. Split delivery into small milestones and identify external teams or systems early.

During implementation, use tests, reviews, contract checks, and observable logging and metrics. Plan data migration, deployment, rollback, documentation, and support ownership before release. After deployment, verify real behavior against acceptance criteria and production metrics. Communicate risks early and propose options rather than waiting for perfect information. Ownership also means saying when a requirement is unsafe or unnecessarily complex.

## Common mistakes

- Equating ownership with doing every task alone
- Starting implementation before resolving key assumptions
- Considering the work finished when the pull request merges
- Reporting a risk without proposing options or next steps

## Key terms

- acceptance criteria — observable conditions that define success
- non-functional requirement — quality constraint such as latency or reliability
- decision record — short explanation of a technical choice
- operational ownership — responsibility after deployment
- blast radius — scope affected when a change fails

questions.title: Node.js under concurrent load
questions.prompt: How can a Node.js process handle many concurrent requests if JavaScript runs on one main thread?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain asynchronous I/O, the event loop, what still blocks, and how to use multiple cores.

expectedPoints.label: Explains asynchronous I/O
expectedPoints.description: I/O waits outside the JavaScript call stack while the process can handle other ready work.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the event loop
expectedPoints.description: The event loop schedules ready callbacks and promise continuations on the main JavaScript thread.

expectedPoints.label: Names blocking limits
expectedPoints.description: CPU-heavy or synchronous work blocks progress and should be moved, split, or isolated.

expectedPoints.label: Uses processes or containers for cores
expectedPoints.description: Multiple processes or replicas use multiple cores and improve isolation.

followUps.prompt: What metrics would show that the event loop is becoming a bottleneck?

questions.title: CPU-heavy work in a request handler
questions.prompt: A Node.js endpoint performs a CPU-heavy calculation and other requests become slow. How would you redesign it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Confirm the bottleneck, then compare worker threads, background jobs, another service, and flow control.

expectedPoints.label: Confirms the cause with measurement
expectedPoints.description: Uses CPU profiling and event-loop delay rather than assuming the calculation is the cause.

expectedPoints.label: Moves CPU work off the main thread
expectedPoints.description: Uses worker threads, a background queue, or a separate compute service.

expectedPoints.label: Designs asynchronous completion
expectedPoints.description: Returns a job identifier or status and avoids holding an HTTP request open for long work.

expectedPoints.label: Controls capacity and backpressure
expectedPoints.description: Limits concurrency and protects the system from more work than it can process.

followUps.prompt: When would you choose a worker thread instead of a separate service?

questions.title: Diagnosing a Node.js memory leak
questions.prompt: A long-running Node.js service uses more memory every day and eventually restarts. How would you investigate it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Separate heap from total memory, gather a timeline, compare snapshots, find retaining references, and verify with a soak test.

expectedPoints.label: Builds a memory timeline
expectedPoints.description: Correlates heap, RSS, garbage collection, traffic, and restarts over time.

expectedPoints.label: Compares heap snapshots
expectedPoints.description: Takes comparable snapshots and examines growing object types and retaining paths.

expectedPoints.label: Checks common retention causes
expectedPoints.description: Looks for unbounded caches, listeners, timers, closures, unresolved work, or buffers.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies the fix under sustained load
expectedPoints.description: Repeats the reproduction and runs a soak test to prove memory stabilizes.

followUps.prompt: How would you distinguish a JavaScript heap leak from large Buffer or native memory usage?

questions.title: Diagnosing rising API latency
questions.prompt: An API's p95 latency doubled after a release, but average CPU is still low. How would you find the cause?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare before and after, use percentiles and traces, inspect event-loop delay and dependencies, then verify one change at a time.

expectedPoints.label: Scopes the regression
expectedPoints.description: Identifies affected endpoints, versions, time window, traffic, payload, and error changes.

expectedPoints.label: Breaks latency into components
expectedPoints.description: Uses traces and dependency timing to locate database, network, queue, or application delay.

expectedPoints.label: Checks Node.js-specific signals
expectedPoints.description: Examines event-loop delay, garbage collection, CPU profiles, active handles, and connection pools.

expectedPoints.label: Uses tail percentiles and validation
expectedPoints.description: Focuses on p95/p99 and confirms the fix against the original SLO.

followUps.prompt: Why can low average CPU still coexist with high latency in Node.js?

questions.title: Async error handling in a backend service
questions.prompt: How do you structure error handling for promises and async/await in a Node.js API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover propagation, boundary handling, consistent error responses, logging, and process-level failures.

expectedPoints.label: Explains async error propagation
expectedPoints.description: Rejected promises must be awaited or returned so errors reach a catch or framework boundary.

expectedPoints.label: Handles errors at clear boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Central middleware maps internal errors to stable HTTP responses without exposing secrets.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies expected and unexpected errors
expectedPoints.description: Separates validation, not-found, conflict, dependency, and programming failures.

expectedPoints.label: Logs context and preserves failure policy
expectedPoints.description: Uses correlation data and decides whether an unrecoverable process state should restart.

followUps.prompt: What is dangerous about catching an error and returning a fallback value without logging or rethrowing it?

questions.title: Designing a maintainable REST API
questions.prompt: What makes a REST API maintainable for internal and external consumers?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss resource design, HTTP semantics, validation, stable contracts, compatibility, and documentation.

expectedPoints.label: Uses clear resource and HTTP semantics
expectedPoints.description: Chooses stable resource names, methods, status codes, and idempotent behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Defines and validates the contract
expectedPoints.description: Documents request, response, errors, pagination, and validation with a machine-readable specification.

expectedPoints.label: Plans compatibility
expectedPoints.description: Uses additive changes, deprecation, versioning only when needed, and consumer communication.

expectedPoints.label: Includes operational concerns
expectedPoints.description: Covers authentication, authorization, rate limiting, tracing, and timeouts.

followUps.prompt: Give an example of an API change that looks harmless but can break an existing consumer.

questions.title: GraphQL trade-offs and the N+1 problem
questions.prompt: When would GraphQL be useful, and how would you prevent a GraphQL API from overloading the backend?
questions.answerStructureHint: State the benefit, then discuss N+1, batching, query limits, authorization, caching, and when REST is simpler.

expectedPoints.label: Explains when GraphQL fits
expectedPoints.description: Highlights client-selected fields, graph traversal, and reducing multiple client round trips.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents N+1 queries
expectedPoints.description: Uses batching and per-request caching, such as DataLoader, and verifies database behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Controls query cost
expectedPoints.description: Applies depth, complexity, pagination, timeout, and rate controls.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes operational trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Mentions field-level authorization, caching difficulty, monitoring, and cases where REST is simpler.

followUps.prompt: Why is a resolver that looks simple in code still able to generate hundreds of SQL queries?

questions.title: Choosing SQL or NoSQL
questions.prompt: How would you choose between PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and Redis for a new backend feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Start with access patterns and consistency, then compare relational constraints, document flexibility, and Redis use cases.

expectedPoints.label: Starts from data requirements
expectedPoints.description: Considers access patterns, relationships, consistency, transactions, scale, and query evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Explains PostgreSQL strengths
expectedPoints.description: Uses relational constraints, joins, transactions, and flexible querying for core durable data.

expectedPoints.label: Explains MongoDB strengths and limits
expectedPoints.description: Fits aggregate documents and evolving shape but still requires indexing and consistency design.

expectedPoints.label: Positions Redis correctly
expectedPoints.description: Uses Redis for cache, ephemeral state, counters, locks, or fast access, not by default as the only durable database.

followUps.prompt: What data would make you reject MongoDB even if the schema changes frequently?

questions.title: A slow database-backed endpoint
questions.prompt: A list endpoint becomes slow as the table grows. What would you inspect and change?
questions.answerStructureHint: Measure the query, inspect its plan and indexes, reduce work and payload, choose pagination, then check pool and lock pressure.

expectedPoints.label: Inspects the real query and plan
expectedPoints.description: Uses slow-query data and EXPLAIN to identify scans, joins, sorts, and row estimates.

expectedPoints.label: Designs indexes from predicates and ordering
expectedPoints.description: Adds or adjusts selective indexes and validates write and storage cost.

expectedPoints.label: Reduces rows and payload
expectedPoints.description: Uses projection, bounded page size, and suitable cursor pagination instead of loading everything.

expectedPoints.label: Checks contention and pool health
expectedPoints.description: Examines locks, transaction duration, pool saturation, and downstream serialization.

followUps.prompt: Why can adding an index make some workloads worse?

questions.title: Updating a database and publishing an event
questions.prompt: A service must save an order and publish OrderCreated. How do you avoid a state where only one of those actions succeeds?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain the dual-write problem, transactional outbox, relay, retries, idempotent consumers, and monitoring.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies the dual-write problem
expectedPoints.description: A database commit and broker publish cannot normally be one atomic local operation.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a transactional outbox
expectedPoints.description: Writes the business change and outbox record in one database transaction.

expectedPoints.label: Publishes reliably from the outbox
expectedPoints.description: A relay polls or streams committed outbox rows, publishes, and records completion with retries.

expectedPoints.label: Handles duplicate delivery and operations
expectedPoints.description: Consumers are idempotent, and backlog, failures, and age are monitored.

followUps.prompt: What failure can still create a duplicate event even with an outbox pattern?

questions.title: Queue, publish-subscribe, or request-reply
questions.prompt: Compare queue-based work distribution, publish-subscribe, and request-reply messaging. When would you use each?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe who receives each message, whether a reply is required, and the coupling and failure implications.

expectedPoints.label: Explains queue semantics
expectedPoints.description: One competing consumer handles each task, enabling work distribution and buffering.

expectedPoints.label: Explains publish-subscribe
expectedPoints.description: Each independent subscription receives an event copy for fan-out.

expectedPoints.label: Explains request-reply
expectedPoints.description: A requester correlates a response and accepts temporal coupling or timeout handling.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes commands and events
expectedPoints.description: A command targets an owner; an event announces a completed fact.

followUps.prompt: What goes wrong if a business event is placed on one competing-consumer queue but three services need it?

questions.title: Choosing NATS, RabbitMQ, or Kafka
questions.prompt: How would you choose between NATS, RabbitMQ, and Kafka for an integration platform?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare retention, replay, routing, ordering, throughput, delivery, and operational cost before naming a platform.

expectedPoints.label: Starts with workload criteria
expectedPoints.description: Asks about retention, replay, routing, ordering, throughput, latency, and operations.

expectedPoints.label: Positions NATS
expectedPoints.description: Uses NATS for lightweight subject-based low-latency messaging and mentions JetStream for persistence.

expectedPoints.label: Positions RabbitMQ
expectedPoints.description: Uses RabbitMQ for work queues, acknowledgements, and flexible exchange routing.

expectedPoints.label: Positions Kafka
expectedPoints.description: Uses Kafka for retained high-throughput streams, partitioned ordering, consumer groups, and replay.

followUps.prompt: Why might Kafka be a poor choice for a small low-volume task queue?

questions.title: Duplicates and delivery guarantees
questions.prompt: What do at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once mean, and how would you design a payment-like consumer?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define the semantics, explain the acknowledgement failure window, and design idempotency around a stable operation key.

expectedPoints.label: Defines the delivery semantics
expectedPoints.description: Correctly distinguishes possible loss, possible duplicates, and limited exactly-once boundaries.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the acknowledgement failure window
expectedPoints.description: Processing may succeed before the acknowledgement, causing redelivery.

expectedPoints.label: Designs idempotent processing
expectedPoints.description: Uses a stable operation identifier and atomic deduplication or a unique business constraint.

expectedPoints.label: Adds a bounded retry policy
expectedPoints.description: Classifies errors, backs off, and sends permanent failures to controlled handling.

followUps.prompt: Why does a broker's exactly-once feature not automatically make an external HTTP payment call exactly once?

questions.title: Preserving order without losing scalability
questions.prompt: Events for the same account must be processed in order, but different accounts should run in parallel. How would you design the routing?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define the ordering scope, use the account as a key, keep one ordered stream per key, and handle duplicates and stale events.

expectedPoints.label: Defines per-entity ordering
expectedPoints.description: Limits the guarantee to events for the same account instead of requesting global order.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a stable partition or routing key
expectedPoints.description: Routes the same account to the same partition or ordered consumer stream.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves parallelism across keys
expectedPoints.description: Different accounts are distributed across partitions and consumers.

expectedPoints.label: Handles stale and repeated events
expectedPoints.description: Uses sequence or version checks and idempotent processing.

followUps.prompt: What trade-off appears if all events are forced through one partition?

questions.title: Handling poison messages
questions.prompt: A consumer keeps failing on the same message and blocks useful work. What should the retry and dead-letter flow look like?
questions.answerStructureHint: Classify the error, retry transient failures with limits, preserve diagnostics, alert, and provide safe replay or compensation.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies retryable errors
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes transient failures from permanent validation or schema failures.

expectedPoints.label: Uses bounded backoff
expectedPoints.description: Applies attempt or time limits with backoff and jitter instead of an infinite loop.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves diagnostic context
expectedPoints.description: Stores payload reference, error, attempts, timestamps, and trace identifiers.

expectedPoints.label: Owns monitoring and recovery
expectedPoints.description: Alerts on age and volume and supports throttled, idempotent replay or compensation.

followUps.prompt: Why should replay use a controlled tool instead of simply moving every message back at once?

questions.title: Evolving an event contract
questions.prompt: You need to rename a required field in an event consumed by several independently deployed services. How would you roll out the change?
questions.answerStructureHint: Avoid the direct rename, introduce an additive transition or a new version, migrate consumers, and keep replay compatibility.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes the direct rename as breaking
expectedPoints.description: Old consumers and historical events still expect the original field.

expectedPoints.label: Uses an additive migration
expectedPoints.description: Publishes both fields or adds an optional replacement while consumers migrate.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a new version for incompatible meaning
expectedPoints.description: Introduces a new event type or major version if compatibility cannot be preserved.

expectedPoints.label: Adds schema and contract checks
expectedPoints.description: Uses registry rules, contract tests, and consumer communication before removing old support.

followUps.prompt: What metadata would you include in every event envelope?

questions.title: Designing an event-driven integration
questions.prompt: A new customer registration must update billing, CRM, analytics, and email systems. How would you design the integration?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify required versus optional outcomes, publish a stable event, isolate subscribers, and cover retries, idempotency, ordering, and observability.

expectedPoints.label: Separates required and optional outcomes
expectedPoints.description: Clarifies what must finish before registration succeeds and what may be eventually consistent.

expectedPoints.label: Publishes a stable business event
expectedPoints.description: Uses a CustomerRegistered fact with a clear owner, schema, identifier, and version.

expectedPoints.label: Creates independent subscriptions
expectedPoints.description: Each downstream system has isolated consumption, retry, and scaling behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Covers distributed failure modes
expectedPoints.description: Uses outbox, idempotency, dead-letter handling, tracing, and backlog monitoring.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if billing must succeed before registration is considered complete?

questions.title: Synchronous or asynchronous service integration
questions.prompt: Two microservices need to exchange data. How do you decide between a REST call and asynchronous messaging?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare immediacy, consistency, availability, coupling, buffering, failure handling, and operational complexity.

expectedPoints.label: Explains synchronous fit
expectedPoints.description: Uses REST when an immediate response is required and the dependency can be part of the request path.

expectedPoints.label: Explains asynchronous fit
expectedPoints.description: Uses messaging for buffering, fan-out, independent availability, and eventual consistency.

expectedPoints.label: Discusses failure behavior
expectedPoints.description: Covers timeouts and circuit breakers for sync, retries and duplicates for async.

expectedPoints.label: Maintains ownership and contracts
expectedPoints.description: Avoids shared databases and defines stable API or event contracts.

followUps.prompt: Can asynchronous messaging still create tight coupling? Give an example.

questions.title: When Azure Functions are a good fit
questions.prompt: For what kinds of backend components would you choose Azure Functions, and when would you prefer a long-running service?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare triggers, traffic shape, latency, execution duration, state, operations, and cost.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies suitable function workloads
expectedPoints.description: Mentions bursty event handlers, timers, queue consumers, and small integration tasks.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes unsuitable workloads
expectedPoints.description: Calls out long-running, stateful, predictable high-load, or strict low-latency work.

expectedPoints.label: Designs for function behavior
expectedPoints.description: Keeps handlers stateless, idempotent, observable, and tolerant of retries and cold starts.

expectedPoints.label: Compares total operational cost
expectedPoints.description: Considers plans, pre-warming, networking, debugging, vendor coupling, and team experience.

followUps.prompt: How could a queue-triggered function accidentally process the same message more than once?

questions.title: A safe CI/CD pipeline for backend services
questions.prompt: What stages and controls would you put in a CI/CD pipeline for Node.js services and message consumers?
questions.answerStructureHint: Build once, validate at several levels, manage configuration and migrations, deploy gradually, observe, and recover.

expectedPoints.label: Builds and promotes one artifact
expectedPoints.description: Produces an immutable versioned artifact and promotes it through environments.

expectedPoints.label: Uses layered quality gates
expectedPoints.description: Includes unit, integration, contract, security, and content or configuration validation.

expectedPoints.label: Handles compatibility and migrations
expectedPoints.description: Uses backward-compatible API, event, and database changes with staged rollout.

expectedPoints.label: Limits blast radius and recovers
expectedPoints.description: Uses health checks, canary or blue-green deployment, metrics, and explicit rollback criteria.

followUps.prompt: Why can rolling back application code be unsafe after a database migration?

questions.title: Handling a production incident
questions.prompt: A message consumer is falling behind and customers report delayed updates. Walk through your response.
questions.answerStructureHint: Stabilize impact, inspect lag and saturation, find the bottleneck, communicate, recover safely, and follow up.

expectedPoints.label: Stabilizes customer impact
expectedPoints.description: Assesses severity, pauses harmful changes, limits intake or scales safe capacity, and defines ownership.

expectedPoints.label: Uses broker and service signals
expectedPoints.description: Checks consumer lag, throughput, errors, retries, partitions, CPU, event-loop delay, and dependencies.

expectedPoints.label: Recovers without creating more damage
expectedPoints.description: Fixes or rolls forward, controls replay, preserves ordering, and watches backlog drain.

expectedPoints.label: Communicates and prevents recurrence
expectedPoints.description: Provides status updates, records a timeline, and creates measured corrective actions.

followUps.prompt: What would make simply adding more consumer replicas ineffective?

questions.title: Maintaining a .NET component from a Node.js team
questions.prompt: Your Node.js service depends on an existing .NET/C# component that also needs maintenance. How would you approach the boundary and reduce risk?
questions.answerStructureHint: Understand the current behavior, stabilize the contract, add tests and observability, then improve or replace incrementally.

expectedPoints.label: Maps the existing behavior and ownership
expectedPoints.description: Documents interfaces, data ownership, dependencies, deployment, and failure modes before changing it.

expectedPoints.label: Creates a stable boundary
expectedPoints.description: Uses an API, message contract, or adapter rather than sharing internal classes or uncontrolled database access.

expectedPoints.label: Adds tests and observability
expectedPoints.description: Uses characterization and contract tests plus logs, metrics, and tracing around the component.

expectedPoints.label: Improves incrementally
expectedPoints.description: Uses a strangler approach, small ownership transfers, comparison, and rollback instead of a blind rewrite.

followUps.prompt: When is keeping the .NET component a better decision than rewriting it in Node.js?

questions.title: Prioritizing technical debt
questions.prompt: How do you decide which technical debt to fix when feature work is also urgent?
questions.answerStructureHint: Translate debt into impact, rank risk and frequency, choose incremental action, and communicate the consequence of delay.

expectedPoints.label: Expresses debt as measurable impact
expectedPoints.description: Connects it to incidents, security, delivery delay, support cost, or scaling limits.

expectedPoints.label: Ranks by risk and frequency
expectedPoints.description: Considers likelihood, severity, how often the area changes, and the cost of waiting.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses proportionate remediation
expectedPoints.description: Fixes urgent risk now and uses incremental refactoring or feature-linked cleanup for the rest.

expectedPoints.label: Communicates options and verifies value
expectedPoints.description: Explains trade-offs in business terms and checks whether incidents or lead time improve.

followUps.prompt: Give an example of technical debt you would deliberately accept for a limited time.

questions.title: Taking a feature from requirements to production
questions.prompt: What does project ownership look like when you take a backend feature from an initial request through production and maintenance?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify outcomes and constraints, design and coordinate, deliver in safe increments, prepare operations, and verify production results.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies the problem and success criteria
expectedPoints.description: Identifies users, acceptance criteria, edge cases, NFRs, and unresolved assumptions.

expectedPoints.label: Makes and communicates design decisions
expectedPoints.description: Documents contracts, ownership, alternatives, risks, and dependencies with the team.

expectedPoints.label: Plans safe incremental delivery
expectedPoints.description: Splits work, coordinates external teams, tests, reviews, migrates, and deploys gradually.

expectedPoints.label: Owns production behavior
expectedPoints.description: Adds observability and documentation, verifies outcomes after release, and responds to issues.

followUps.prompt: How do you handle a requirement that is still ambiguous but the deadline cannot move?

questions.title: Code review and technical documentation
questions.prompt: What do you look for in a backend code review, and what documentation should accompany a new integration?
questions.answerStructureHint: Review correctness and design, failure handling and security, tests and operations, then document contracts and deployment.

expectedPoints.label: Reviews correctness and maintainability
expectedPoints.description: Checks behavior, edge cases, readability, boundaries, and unnecessary complexity.

expectedPoints.label: Reviews failure and security behavior
expectedPoints.description: Checks validation, authorization, timeouts, retries, idempotency, and secret handling.

expectedPoints.label: Reviews evidence and operations
expectedPoints.description: Looks for proportionate tests, observability, migration safety, and deployment impact.

expectedPoints.label: Documents the integration contract
expectedPoints.description: Provides API or event schemas, examples, error behavior, dependencies, configuration, and runbooks.

followUps.prompt: How would you give review feedback when the code works but the design creates long-term coupling?
